claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the child or children you're asking about.

Let me start with a few basic questions:

1. **Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me their names and ages?

Once I know about the children in your care, I'll ask some follow-up questions about your specific situation to determine eligibility for each child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have three children I'm responsible for. There's Alex, who's 4 years old, Blake who's 16, and Charlie who's 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have three children:
- Alex, age 4
- Blake, age 16
- Charlie, age 15

Now I need to ask some questions about each child to assess eligibility. Let me start with some general questions:

**First, do all three children normally live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex and Charlie live with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me anymore.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Alex and Charlie live with you, but Blake doesn't.

Let me ask about Blake first, since their living situation is different:

**Where does Blake live now? For example:**
- Does Blake live with another parent or family member?
- Is Blake in full-time education or training?
- Are you still financially responsible for Blake in any way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake moved out recently but is still figuring things out. They just left school and signed up with the careers service to work out what to do next. I still help out financially - I give them money each week for their expenses.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake. That's helpful to know they've registered with the careers service and you're still supporting them financially.

**A few more questions about Blake:**

1. **When exactly did Blake move out?** (I need to know the specific date or at least how many weeks ago)

2. **Where is Blake living now?** (For example, with friends, in their own accommodation, with another relative, etc.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake moved out about 3 months ago - they're living with their dad now. I still contribute to their upkeep though.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So Blake has been living with their dad for about 3 months, and you're still contributing financially.

**One important question about Blake: Does Blake's dad receive Child Benefit for Blake, or is he planning to claim it?**

Now, let me ask about your other two children:

**For Alex (age 4) and Charlie (age 15):**
- Are they both in full-time education (or in Alex's case, not yet in school)?
- Are either of them in hospital or in local authority care for any extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake's dad doesn't claim Child Benefit for Blake - I still contribute money towards Blake's upkeep each week.

As for Alex and Charlie - Alex isn't in school yet, he's only 4. Charlie is in secondary school. 

Alex has actually been in hospital for a few months now, but I'm still very much involved in his care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a few more questions to get a complete picture:

**About Alex's hospital stay:**
- How long has Alex been in hospital? (You mentioned a few months - could you tell me approximately how many weeks or months?)
- Do you visit Alex regularly in hospital?

**About Charlie:**
- Is Charlie in full-time education at secondary school?
- Does Charlie live with you full-time without any issues?

**About Blake:**
- Since Blake is 16 and has left school, even though they're registered with the careers service, this is an important detail. Are they enrolled in any approved training or education programme, or are they currently just registered and waiting to start something?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
**About Alex:**
It's been about 12 weeks now that he's been in hospital. Yes, I visit him regularly and I'm still paying for things he needs.

**About Charlie:**
Yes, Charlie's in full-time secondary school and lives with me at home. No issues there at all.

**About Blake:**
Blake's just registered with the careers service - they're not in any training or education programme yet, just figuring out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that detail. Let me make sure I have everything clear:

**Alex (age 4):** Has been in hospital for about 12 weeks. You visit regularly and pay for things they need.

**Charlie (age 15):** Lives with you, in full-time secondary school, no issues.

**Blake (age 16):** Left school, registered with careers service but not yet in any education or training programme, living with their dad for about 3 months (approximately 12 weeks), and you still contribute financially.

Just a couple of final questions:

1. **Are you (the claimant) currently living in the UK and responsible for these children?**

2. **For Blake specifically:** Before Blake moved to their dad's 3 months ago, were you claiming Child Benefit for Blake at that time?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

As for Blake, I'm not currently claiming Child Benefit for any of them - that's why I'm asking about my eligibility now. I want to know if I can claim it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! So you're in the UK and you're asking about whether you can claim Child Benefit for all three children.

Let me ask one final important question:

**For Blake specifically:** Since Blake now lives with their dad, I need to understand the care arrangement better. Even though you contribute financially, in practical terms:
- Does Blake's dad provide the main day-to-day care for Blake (housing, meals, etc.)?
- Or would you say you and Blake's dad share the responsibility equally?

This is important because when a child doesn't live with you, the person who has the main responsibility can usually claim Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake's dad provides the main day-to-day care - Blake lives there full-time with him. I just help out with money for Blake's expenses each week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have a complete picture of your situation. Let me assess the eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## My Assessment of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment for each of your three children:

---

### **Charlie (age 15) - ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You **are eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie is under 16, lives with you full-time, and is in full-time secondary education. You meet all the requirements.

---

### **Alex (age 4) - ✗ NOT CURRENTLY ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex normally lives with you and is the right age, Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks. Under Child Benefit rules, you can only continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital for up to **12 weeks (84 days)**. Since Alex has been hospitalized for around 12 weeks, you have reached or exceeded this time limit. You would not be eligible while Alex remains in hospital beyond this period.

**Important:** Once Alex comes home from hospital, you should be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex again.

---

### **Blake (age 16) - ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**
You are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake for two reasons:

1. **Blake doesn't live with you** - Blake lives with their dad who provides the main day-to-day care. The person the child lives with and who has main responsibility is the one who can claim.

2. **Blake is not in qualifying education or training** - Blake has left school and is only registered with the careers service. For young people aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training for Child Benefit to continue. Simply being registered with the careers service doesn't qualify.

---

### **What You Should Do:**

✓ **You can apply for Child Benefit for Charlie** right away.

If Blake's dad wanted to claim for Blake, Blake would need to be enrolled in approved education or training (not just registered with careers service).

Once Alex comes home from hospital, you should be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex again.

Do you have any questions about this assessment?

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old and normally lives with you, which would typically make you eligible. However, Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks (about 3 months). Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can only continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital for up to 12 weeks (84 days). Since Alex has been in hospital for around 12 weeks, you have either reached or exceeded this time limit. Therefore, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex while they remain in hospital beyond the 12-week period.

Blake is 16 years old and has left school. Blake is not currently in approved education or training - they are only registered with the careers service. Additionally, Blake does not live with you; Blake lives with their dad who provides the main day-to-day care. Under Child Benefit rules, the person who the child lives with and who has main responsibility can claim. Since Blake's dad has main responsibility and provides day-to-day care, he would be the person eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake (if Blake were in qualifying education or training). You are not eligible to claim for Blake because: (1) Blake doesn't live with you, and (2) Blake is not in approved education or training.

Charlie is 15 years old, lives with you full-time, and is in full-time secondary school education. You are responsible for Charlie and there are no complicating factors. You meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie: you are responsible for the child, the child lives with you, the child is under 16 (or under 20 and in approved education/training), and you live in the UK.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for three children. They are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie (age 15, lives with them, in full-time education). They are not eligible for Alex because Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, which meets or exceeds the 12-week limit for Child Benefit while a child is hospitalized. They are not eligible for Blake because Blake lives with their dad who provides main day-to-day care, and Blake is not currently in approved education or training despite being over 16.

They are not eligible for Blake because Blake lives with their dad who provides main day-to-day care, and Blake is not currently in approved education or training despite being over 16.
